title: Revisiting the Motivic Cohomology of Schemes
speaker: Matthew Morrow
abstract: Since the original conjectures of Beilinson and Lichtenbaum in the 80s, several versions of motivic cohomology have been introduced and developed, notably by Voevodsky. Most classically, Bloch's higher Chow groups provide the accepted theory of motivic cohomology for smooth algebraic varieties, simultaneously related to algebraic cycles, étale cohomology, and algebraic K-theory.
tags: Special Year Workshop on p-adic Arithmetic Geometry

proof_stages.label: Beilinson–Lichtenbaum desiderata for motivic complexes
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up motivic complexes in nonnegative weights and recalls the expected finite-coefficient étale/syntomic comparison, spectral sequence to algebraic K-theory, and rational control by K-theory.

proof_stages.label: A1-invariant replacement and smooth benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The original problem is weakened by forcing A1-invariance, replacing K-theory by homotopy invariant K-theory KH, and then comparing this with the classical Bloch–Friedlander–Levine–Suslin–Voevodsky theory for smooth schemes.

proof_stages.label: QCQS A1-motivic complexes: CDH and syntomic formulas
proof_stages.summary: For arbitrary QCQS schemes, the new theory is stated through CDH descriptions in weights 0 and 1 and through a mod-p formula obtained from truncated syntomic cohomology after CDH sheafification and A1-localization.

proof_stages.label: Formal extension from smooth schemes and hidden constraints
proof_stages.summary: The complexes are constructed by left Kan extending smooth motivic cohomology to all schemes, then imposing CDH descent and A1-invariance, with the main hidden work being projective bundle compatibility and control via p-adic cohomology and localizing invariants.

proof_stages.label: Stable motivic homotopy category as representing setting
proof_stages.summary: The stable motivic homotopy category SH(X), its motive functor, associated weight-j cohomology theories, P1-bundle compatibility, and the representing objects for A1-motivic cohomology and KH are introduced.

proof_stages.label: Slice filtration and Voevodsky's zero-slice program
proof_stages.summary: The slice filtration is motivated by effectivity and absence of negative weights, yielding spectral sequences and Voevodsky's conjecture that the zero slice of KGL equals the zero slice of the unit and represents A1-motivic cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Verification and consequences for motives
proof_stages.summary: The theorem with Bachmann and Elmanto proves Voevodsky's zero-slice conjectures for every QCQS scheme, giving pullback compatibility and hence a six-functor formalism for modules over motivic cohomology, with Q&A noting trace-to-TC applications and boundedness issues.

examples.name: Atiyah–Hirzebruch analogy
examples.note: The motivic spectral sequence to algebraic K-theory is compared to the topological Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence, serving only to orient the desired relationship with K-theory.
examples.engagement_reason: He only invokes the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence as a topological analogy/name for the motivic spectral sequence, without working through that analogy.

passes.reason: The Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral-sequence analogy is a standard systematic construction used explanatorily, not as a failure case.
The Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence is invoked only as a standard analogy for the motivic spectral sequence, not as a structurally special object or a failure case.
Standard Atiyah–Hirzebruch-type spectral sequence used only as a naming analogy, with no special structure invoked
the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral-sequence analogy is used as a standard positive comparison between motivic cohomology and K-theory, not as a special object or refutation
the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence is invoked as a standard analogy/model for the motivic spectral sequence, not as a special or adversarial object
The Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral-sequence analogy is a standard systematic comparison, not a structurally special object or a refutation.
the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence is invoked as a standard topological analogy for the motivic spectral sequence, not as an obstruction or structurally special object
The Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral-sequence analogy is used only to orient the motivic-to-K-theory spectral sequence, not to refute a claim, and the spectral sequence is structurally ordinary.
The Atiyah–Hirzebruch analogy is used only to identify the motivic spectral sequence relating motivic cohomology to K-theory, with no special intrinsic structure invoked.
the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence is invoked as a standard analogy for the motivic spectral sequence, not as an obstruction or special object
the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence is invoked only as a standard analogy for the motivic spectral sequence
The Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence is a standard systematic spectral sequence used only as an analogy for the motivic one.

examples.name: Nilpotent thickenings as CDH-invisible
examples.note: In response to a question, finitely generated nilpotent ideals are mentioned to illustrate why CDH sheaves are nilinvariant.
examples.engagement_reason: He explains why CDH sheaves are nilinvariant for finitely generated nil ideals, reasoning that blowing up a nil ideal does not change anything.

passes.reason: A nilpotent thickening is an infinitesimal/collapsed modification with the same underlying topological space, used to illustrate CDH nilinvariance rather than refute a claim.
Nilpotent thickenings are infinitesimal/collapsed modifications with no new underlying topological space, used to illustrate CDH nil-invariance rather than to refute a claim.
Nilpotent thickenings have a collapsed underlying reduced space and illustrate CDH nilinvariance rather than refuting a claim
nilpotent thickenings are infinitesimal nonreduced/blurred cases whose extra nilpotent structure is collapsed by CDH sheaves, and they are not used to refute a claim
a nilpotent thickening is an infinitesimal nonreduced/collapsed geometric modification with no new underlying topological points, used to illustrate CDH nilinvariance
Nilpotent thickenings are infinitesimal/collapsed nonreduced situations that CDH sheaves ignore, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
a nilpotent thickening is an infinitesimal/nonreduced collapsed case with the same underlying space, used here to illustrate CDH nilinvariance
Nilpotent thickenings are used to illustrate CDH nilinvariance, and as infinitesimal thickenings with no new underlying topological locus they are structurally degenerate.
Nilpotent thickenings are infinitesimal/nonreduced degenerations invisible to CDH sheaves, used here to illustrate nilinvariance rather than to refute a claim.
a nilpotent thickening has the same underlying reduced space and is collapsed/invisible for CDH sheaves, but is used only to explain nilinvariance
nilpotent thickenings are infinitesimal/collapsed nonreduced cases that CDH sheaves ignore
Nilpotent thickenings are infinitesimal nonreduced degenerations collapsed by CDH sheaves, used to illustrate nilinvariance rather than refute a claim.

examples.name: Cases where A1-localization is redundant
examples.note: Schemes over fields, Artinian rings, perfectoid valuation rings, and schemes over Z[1/p] are listed as concrete settings in which the syntomic/CDH expression is already A1-invariant.
examples.engagement_reason: He lists concrete classes where the A1-localization can be removed and explains, for example away from p, how syntomic collapses to étale and CDH descent lets one erase the localization.

passes.reason: The listed bases where A1-localization is redundant are broad systematic cases used as positive instances of the theorem.
The listed schemes where A1-localization can be omitted form systematic positive cases of the theorem, not sporadic, extremal, symmetric, or adversarial examples.
These are systematic positive base cases where A1-localization can be omitted, not structurally sporadic or adversarial
the listed settings where A1-localization is unnecessary are positive families of cases rather than structurally extremal, symmetric, sporadic, or degenerate objects
the listed settings are systematic positive cases where CDH sheafification already gives A1-invariance, not classification-exceptional or counterexample cases
The listed schemes where A1-localization is redundant are systematic positive cases of a theorem, not sporadic or counterexamples.
the listed situations are systematic positive cases where the A1-localization functor can be omitted, not counterexamples or sporadic/extreme objects
The listed hypotheses where A1-localization is redundant are positive cases of a theorem rather than counterexamples, and they form systematic families rather than sporadic or extremal objects.
The listed redundancy cases are broad theorem families where A1-localization is unnecessary, not sporadic, extreme, or adversarial examples.
the listed bases are systematic positive cases where A1-localization is unnecessary, not sporadic, extremal, symmetric, or adversarial examples
the listed hypotheses are positive theorem cases where the extra localization is unnecessary
The listed situations are systematic families where A1-localization is theorematically redundant, not sporadic, extremal, symmetric, or adversarial cases.

examples.name: Cucumber-slicing metaphor
examples.note: The repeated zero-slice construction after Tate shifts is explained by the everyday image of slicing a cucumber, making the slice filtration visually memorable.
examples.engagement_reason: He develops the cucumber-slicing picture step by step to model the slice filtration: move by the Tate motive, slice, move back, producing graded pieces.

passes.reason: The cucumber slicing is only a metaphor for the slice filtration, not a determinate mathematical object to classify structurally.
The cucumber is a non-mathematical metaphor for the slice filtration, so there is no mathematical object to structurally classify.
The cucumber slicing is a non-mathematical metaphor rather than an identifiable mathematical object
